> Doesn’t that show that on a privacy POV, XMR is better than Zcash?

brother, it shows it has network effects amongst a set of criminals, the same way tron has network effects amongst LATAM grocery stores. says nothing about tech. crime is not the main use case of privacy and criminals are not the arbiters of technology. many criminals also still use tg over signal.

btc is dominant on the darkweb, does this imply btc has better privacy or that it's better known?

once zec is shielded, there is no info you can derive. try it yourself. shield it then move funds and look at an explorer. what do you see? nothing

with xmr ring sigs, this is not the case. it's 15 decoy txs (and you can decrypt this w quantum so all this "crime" will be traced retro)

that's not to say xmr is "not private", but it is not "stronger" in privacy

the reality is

i) zcash was unusable until 2y ago since zk tech was early and needed to advance. that's also why the project saw no movement until 1.5y ago

ii) during that time, xmr was already in the market and aggressively catering towards exactly this crowd for years

iii) it is no different than tron being slower and more expensive than many L1s/L2s but getting disproportionate southeast asia payments volume, it is a network effect, not technical proof that tron is the most usable chain in the market

iv) also, how do you know what people use for crime? you by definition can only see crimes that are not private. such ppl also used encrochat and anom for msging until they got baited into capture. 

v) zcash is about individual liberty for all and the monetary asset and uses the privacy and quantum proofing to strengthen that case. the ethos will never be aiming at this niche because the only way privacy wins is if it's the norm for all, not just a few.

(a federal whistleblower arguably has the highest stakes of all and you can see what snowden uses)

tweets.text:
Fascinating debate and important discussion here! It’s worth separating out the dynamics in the US and in China, where the vast majority of the leading open source models are from. 

In the below post, @zephyr_z9 provides a set of very conservative/upper bound estimates for a final pre-training run but that omits several other key dimensions like data acquisition and cleaning, talent costs, R&D experiments and failures, post-training, and cost of standing up full infra in the US beyond GPUs. 

There’s an alternative way to look at the above. Realistically the only other lab that has come close to catching up with OpenAI and Anthropic is Meta’s MSL, which has done it by throwing an ungodly amount of money at it hiring talents and acquiring compute at any cost. 

So the number “$2-4b” cost of a ticket to enter the game of training frontier models in the US seems about right in that it’s formidable and absolutely points towards an oligopolistic structure due to the sheer scale of the financial capital that’s involved and the physical constraints of total AI researchers and GPUs and power.

thread_tweets.text:
But the question of how open source models can keep up is an entirely different question due to the unique competitive nature in China. It’s no accident that the leading open source models are almost all in China. In China it’s easier to overcome the Jack Ma quote of “if money can solve a problem it’s not really a problem”.

Chinese AI labs face a very different set of constraints: cheaper energy, more abundant AI researchers and the ability to distill and learn from leading US models, but less capable and plenty GPUs hence the need for much greater algorithmic efficiencies and prevalent use of MoE architecture.

On the question of limiting distillation. I’m skeptical on the balance that it’ll be ultimately successful. The top labs are in competition with each other so you have the classic prisoners’ dilemma Nash equilibrium. They cannot help themselves and a collusion is unlikely to sustain even if they liked and trusted each other and they don’t. 

Meanwhile the frontier models are enormously expensive to train and create. If you only release it very narrowly and sparingly with few high value users, the scope of generating enormous revenues would be extremely limited preventing if not precluding the recuperation of investments and further discourages the funding and creation of such models. 

Further, knowledge diffuses even if model access is limited. A large number of AI researchers is Chinese: they move between labs just like all other AI researchers and talk on WeChat in Chinese all the time. Knowledge or knowledge about techniques transfer like heat in a room even with some insulation.

tweets.text:
I don't net short with leverage. Nothing against people who do it well, and there are some that do and are very talented. It's just not my strength and not my style. If I'm bearish I go to cash, or hedge spot a little bit. You will see me disappear during those times bc I'm just not trading. 

The math is why. Longs can go to infinity, they are uncapped on upside where as shorts are capped. Essentially best case it goes to 0 and you make a 2x. 

SNDK did 60x or so in the past year. 50% fall pays you 50% whether you nailed a parabolic top or shorted something like ETH which has already been cut in half if it fell in half now. The short doesn't care how extended the move was. People who've been trying to call tops with levered shorts, well, it's typically an ego thing. More often than not. it's better to short the thing that has been performing like crap anyways. idk what the obsession  is with shorting strength repeatedly 

Then in equities you have other things that go against you. It's carry. Shorts bleed, you pay the borrow, pay the dividend, fighting stock buybacks, inflation, etc. The timing has to be pretty precise. And then there are naked shorts, where you end up shorting something and end up losing 3-4x the amount you invested. Short MU at $250, it goes to 1k. It happens all the time. 

It is so much better to just have a few good plays, and then every once in awhile you find a high conviction bet to really just push to the max. Even for me, most trades I take are pretty consistent, and once a year I hit a trade that makes up like 70% of my years gains bc I knew it was the one. But that's just my style.

The short doesn't care how extended the move was. People who've been trying to call tops with levered shorts, well, it's typically an ego thing. More often than not. it's better to short the thing that has been performing like crap anyways. idk what the obsession  is with shorting strength repeatedly 

Then in equities you have other things that go against you. It's carry. Shorts bleed, you pay the borrow, pay the dividend, fighting stock buybacks, inflation, etc. The timing has to be pretty precise. And then there are naked shorts, where you end up shorting something and end up losing 3-4x the amount you invested. Short MU at $250, it goes to 1k. It happens all the time. 

It is so much better to just have a few good plays, and then every once in awhile you find a high conviction bet to really just push to the max. Even for me, most trades I take are pretty consistent, and once a year I hit a trade that makes up like 70% of my years gains bc I knew it was the one. But that's just my style.

It’s almost funny how obvious this $LAB scam is, yet nobody seems to be doing anything about it.

The token is up another 2x this week and is now trading at an $8.6 BILLION FDV.

It’s pretty clear that shady market makers are painting the chart to lure in retail traders and then extract liquidity from them. The sad part is that something like this is only possible with the supportof CEXs, since you’d need fee-exempt accounts to pull it off at scale. Otherwise, the costs would be far too high.

For example, if you look at DEXs like PancakeSwap, there’s so little liquidity for a token supposedly worth nearly 11-digits that a $10,000 buy order would move the price by more than 70%. 

That’s absurd.

One thing is certain: there will always be shady founders trying to get rich through scams. That’s just the unfortunate reality of human nature, and it’s a problem that exists in every industry.

What I find even more disappointing is that major industry players like @Gate, @kucoincom, and @bitget are willing to facilitate this kind of activity just to make a quick buck, even though it damages the very industry they operate in. It shows how short-term and purely profit-driven their thinking is without any moral.

Use DEXs or you’re part of the problem.

I believe there’s only a handful of bullish narratives that exist in crypto that can be profitable to speculate on moving forward.

1: privacy

In a world that’s moving towards digital ID’s, CBDCs, where people scan their eyeballs for $50 and share uncomfortable amounts of access and information with AI models on a daily basis, then privacy is primed to be in a speculative bubble.

2: ai

Yes, decentralised AI is important, Tao is important.

Why? closed leading models are driving dangerous vendor lock-in and unchecked security risks.

Networks like Bittensor unleash a global army of incentivised engineers to secure everything, and safeguard privacy.

Which importantly leads to the centre of my Venn diagram…

3: ai privacy

AI privacy will be a vertical likely to see extreme adoption; we’ve already started to see this, but I believe this is only going to grow as a sector.

Great question... 
1) Use the tools daily on actual problems, not just for simple things that the majority of people are using it for. Trying new tools is very important. 

2) Surround yourself with the people that are interested in building... your peer group is your single biggest leading indicator.

 3) I try to stay as close to the source as possible, founders, researchers, primary papers, etc. 

 4) Having a strong MTP. I look at things as "what's now possible?", and try to spread that vision onto others rather than thinking "what's at risk?... 

Things are moving fast, and truly, it's impossible to stay 100% on top of everything, but doing something and at least staying connected is better than not paying attention.

High Conviction Portfolio

About a year ago, my nephew gathered all his savings, and I told him we were going to invest it in the best ideas we could find.

Over the past 12 months, stocks have come and gone from the portfolio, but I believe the current setup is the strongest it’s ever been. The portfolio is up roughly 143% over the last 12 months.

The biggest contributors so far have been $ZIM, $STAA, and $IMDX.

After adding a few recent positions, I’ve decided not to make many changes. I believe this portfolio has the potential to double again over the next 12 months.

The current top three holdings, where I see the most asymmetric opportunities, are $WGS, $ORCL, and $NOW.

There is no leverage and no options. The strategy is simple: make very concentrated bets and allocate at least 30% to my highest-conviction idea at the time.

Most of the current positions were established over the last two to four weeks. Barring a major change in fundamentals, I don’t expect to make many changes from here.

My nephew is young and doesn’t need the money anytime soon, so this is truly a long-term portfolio. I have no intention of trading these positions.

These are my highest-conviction ideas based on where I see the greatest risk/reward opportunities today. Because of concentration, this portfolio can experience significant drawdowns along the way.

It’s designed for someone young with a long time horizon, not for everyone.

tweets.text:
Awareness: The Final Frontier

Close your eyes and ask yourself: Where does awareness begin, and where does it end?

A house has boundaries. Your body has boundaries. Countries have boundaries. But can you find the boundary of awareness?

Most people cannot.

Now open your eyes. The world appears. The sky, the trees, your body, other people. All of it appears in awareness.

Every scientific discovery appears in awareness. The stars, the galaxies, the universe itself. You know them only because you are aware of them.

The same is true of God. Whether God is real or not, your experience, belief, or idea of God appears in awareness.

The same is true of the soul. Whether you believe you have one or not, that belief and experience appear in awareness.

Everything you know of the world, science, God, soul, and self appears in awareness.

But if awareness is truly personal, where are its boundaries?

We say “my awareness” and “your awareness,” but ownership requires a boundary.

A house can be yours because it has boundaries. A body can be yours because it has boundaries.

Can you find where your awareness ends and another person’s begins?

Or have you simply assumed the boundary is there because bodies appear separate?

Now look again. You see other people and you see your own body. But both are known in exactly the same way: as appearances in awareness.

What makes one “me” and the other “other”? Is that distinction directly experienced, or is it inferred?

In a dream, there are people, objects, places, and even a dream version of you. Everything appears separate. Yet upon waking, you realize it all appeared within a single field of consciousness.

The separation appeared real. Whether it was ultimately real is another question.

We spend our lives investigating what appears in awareness, but rarely investigate awareness itself.

Can you find anything outside awareness?

Can you find where the world ends and awareness begins?

Can you find where your awareness ends and mine begins?

Or have you simply assumed those boundaries exist?

Perhaps awareness is the final frontier.

Not what appears within it.

But that in which everything appears.

tweets.text:
Video popped up on my feed:

“You’re not profitable because of what you do outside of trading.”

Lads, the psychology slop genre has gone too far.

I can put a Buddhist monk in front of a terminal - he’s generally not gonna make any money. 

You need some sort of edge, strategy that’s +EV, or even a broad idea of a market effect that you’re trying to monetise. 

There has to be a sensible foundation for the actual trades you take, and then ‘psychology’ can help with reducing unforced errors. 

Most of the trading psychology stuff is cope; it allows you to focus on breathing exercises instead of confronting that your clicking is completely random.

tweets.text:
Many of us who experienced the “Ethereum Killers” of ca 2018 wrote off other blockchains prematurely.

We thought Ethereum would be the only game in town after EOS, Tezos, etc failed.

I held this belief up until 2021. Others took longer to realize they had fooled themselves. Some haven’t even realized yet.

Fortunately for me, I run Nansen so: 1/ I see what people do onchain, and 2/ I hear what chains our users demand from us.

Solana is the clearest case study.

Ethereum maxis should have re-underwritten their “hurr durr ethereum killers” thesis.

Instead Solana was ridiculed for being only for memecoins. (Anyone who has used Solana knows this is a reductionist take.)

Solana is here to stay.

Ethereum is also here to stay, but not as the only game in town.

Now we find ourselves at a point in time when certain people refuse to face another truth:

Hyperliquid is here.

And it is here to stay.

I’m reminded of the people who kept sticking their heads in the sand with Ethereum.

Wake up and smell the coffee.

Clinging to your bags is holding you back.

Use the tech, engage with the ecosystem, be curious.

The only thing that’s always true in crypto is that nothing ever stays the same.

Maximalism is intellectual laziness.

Ethereum is here to stay.
Solana is here to stay.
Hyperliquid is here to stay.

(Alongside a handful other chains.)

Every asset will be tokenized.
Billions of people will be owners.
Chains are the financial fabric of the future.

tweets.text:
anthropic and openai have now each released multi-agent autonomous features over the last week confirming a very strong shift to agentic ai

- codex can now effectively do the job of a 5 person engineering team. it plans, spins up multiple coding flows and just executes. 

- anthropic launched dynamic workflows with opus 4.8 that pretty much does the same thing.

both companies let you do this while… being on your phone. text your agent and it continues working on your desktop at home. 

this shift means we have another series of bottlenecks:

- memory chips and GPUs scarce
- intents. llms need to learn the nuance and context behind the workflows they’re trying to replace.

both startups made it clear enterprise is where the money is so both are starting there.

tweets.text:
People ask me why I don't use agent-native products instead of Gmail or WhatsApp. 

The same misconception happened during the mobile era, where we thought we were going to have a mobile-native mailbox or cloud or CRM. At the end of the day, all web products became mobile-first. The same is happening in the agentic era.

I will keep using my Google Drive and WhatsApp as long as it works for my agent. 

Of course, these products need to step up and build agent-first APIs. For instance, it's very annoying that the WhatsApp CLI doesn't support replying to a message in a thread or other basic functionalities, but I'm confident they will get it done.
